Longitudinal Analysis of Glycaemic Trajectories in Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

Linear Mixed Effects Model (LMM) and Generalised Estimating Equations (GEE) | GET503 Advanced Numerical Data Analysis | DrPH Epidemiology | Universiti Sains Malaysia

Authors

Siti Nurhafizah binti Saharudin

Ahmad Zulfahmi bin Sha’ari

Nazirul Munir bin Abu Hassan

Mohd Fittri Fahmi bin Fauzi

Published

June 10, 2026

Overview & Study Background

Assignment Details

Item Detail
Assignment GET503 Advanced Numerical Data Analysis
Programme Doctor of Public Health (DrPH) Epidemiology
Institution Universiti Sains Malaysia
Methods Linear Mixed Effects Model (LMM) & Generalised Estimating Equations (GEE)
Outcome HbA1c (%), longitudinal, 3 time points
Sample 20,000 T2DM patients × 3 waves = 60,000 observations
Data source Kaggle: T2DM Longitudinal Treatment Outcomes

Background

Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) is a chronic metabolic disease characterised by persistent hyperglycaemia and remains a leading cause of cardiovascular disease, renal failure, and premature mortality worldwide. In Malaysia, T2DM constitutes a growing public health burden, with national prevalence estimates exceeding 18% of the adult population. Glycated haemoglobin (HbA1c), reflecting average blood glucose over the preceding two to three months, is the primary clinical marker used to monitor long-term glycaemic control. Reducing HbA1c through targeted lifestyle and pharmacological interventions is a central goal of T2DM management at both the individual clinical and population levels.

This analysis utilises a longitudinal dataset of 20,000 T2DM patients, each measured at three structured time points, pre-intervention (T1_Pre), mid-intervention (T2_Mid), and post-intervention (T3_Post), capturing a wide range of patient-level characteristics including demographics, lifestyle behaviours, clinical markers, and intervention type. Because each patient contributes three repeated measurements, standard multiple linear regression (MLR) is inappropriate: observations from the same patient are inherently correlated, violating the independence assumption. Two complementary longitudinal methods address this, the Linear Mixed Effects Model (LMM) and Generalised Estimating Equations (GEE), which differ fundamentally in their estimand and interpretation:

  • LMM models subject-specific effects. It directly estimates how each patient’s HbA1c changes over time by incorporating patient-level random effects, making it ideal for understanding individual trajectories and quantifying between-patient heterogeneity.
  • GEE models population-averaged effects. It estimates the expected HbA1c change for an average patient in the population, making its coefficients directly interpretable for public health policy and programme evaluation.

Both analyses follow the REAAAPP workflow (Read → EDA → Assumptions → Analyse → Assess → Present → Publish), ensuring a structured, reproducible, and transparent analytical process.

Research Question & Objectives

Primary Research Question

Among patients with Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, do different intervention strengths (Weak, Standard, Intensive) produce different trajectories of HbA1c change over time, after adjusting for demographic, lifestyle, and clinical covariates?

Primary Objective

To determine whether intervention strength (Weak, Standard, Intensive) produces significantly different HbA1c trajectories over time, using both LMM (subject-specific estimates) and GEE (population-averaged estimates).

Secondary Objectives

  1. To quantify the overall change in HbA1c over time, independent of intervention group or patient characteristics (unconditional growth model: LMM).
  2. To identify patient-level demographic, lifestyle, and clinical factors independently associated with HbA1c in both the LMM and GEE frameworks.
  3. To estimate and compare adjusted mean HbA1c at each time point across intervention groups using estimated marginal means (EMMs), and to determine whether all three intervention groups differ significantly from each other at the study endpoint.
  4. To select the optimal working correlation structure for GEE using the Quasi-likelihood under Independence Criterion (QIC) and evaluate structural sensitivity through cross-structure comparison.

Study Variables

Outcome variable

Variable Type Description
hba1c Continuous HbA1c (%), primary measure of glycaemic control; the dependent variable in all models

Time variable

Variable Type Description
time_step Ordered factor Study time point: T1_Pre (baseline), T2_Mid, T3_Post
time_num Numeric (0/1/2) Numeric encoding of time, required for the random slope model

Primary exposure (intervention)

Variable Type Levels
intervention_strength Ordered factor Weak (ref), Standard, Intensive

Demographic covariates

Variable Type Description
age Continuous Age in years
gender Factor Female (ref), Male

Lifestyle covariates

Variable Type Description
physical_activity_level Ordered factor Low (ref), Moderate, High
diet_score Continuous Dietary quality score; higher = better
sleep_hours Continuous Average daily sleep (hours)

Clinical covariates

Variable Type Description
bmi Continuous Body Mass Index (kg/m²)
medication_adherence Ordered factor Poor (ref), Moderate, Good
comorbidity_level Ordered factor Low (ref), Medium, High

Administrative variables

Variable Type Description
patient_id Factor Unique patient identifier; used as the random-intercept grouping variable in LMM and the cluster ID in GEE

Reference level convention: All ordered factors are set with the worst or least favourable category as the reference. Regression coefficients therefore represent improvements relative to the most clinically unfavourable state. For gender, Female is set as the reference category throughout.

1 Data Preparation & Exploratory Analysis

This section covers the Read and Explore steps of the REAAAPP framework. All packages are loaded upfront, the shared dataset is prepared once, and exploratory analyses are conducted before any modelling. This sequence ensures that any data quality issues are identified and resolved prior to inference.

1.1 R: Package Setup

All required packages are loaded using pacman::p_load(), which installs any missing packages automatically. The combined package set supports data wrangling, EDA, LMM fitting, GEE fitting, post-estimation, and publication-quality visualisation.

Code 
if (!require("pacman")) install.packages("pacman")

pacman::p_load(
  # Data management
  tidyverse, janitor, here,
  # EDA
  skimr, DataExplorer, corrplot,
  # Tables
  gtsummary, gt, kableExtra,
  # Model output
  broom, broom.mixed, broom.helpers,
  # LMM
  lme4, lmerTest, sjPlot, performance, MuMIn,
  # GEE
  geepack,
  # Post-estimation
  emmeans, car, lmtest, MASS,
  # Visualisation
  ggplot2, patchwork, scales,
  # Utilities
  rstatix, purrr
)

# ── Okabe & Ito (2008) colourblind-safe palette ──────────────
cb_intervention <- c(
  "Weak"      = "#E69F00",
  "Standard"  = "#56B4E9",
  "Intensive" = "#0072B2"
)
cb_time <- c(
  "T1_Pre"  = "#56B4E9",
  "T2_Mid"  = "#009E73",
  "T3_Post" = "#0072B2"
)

# ── Shared ggplot2 theme ──────────────────────────────────────
theme_gee <- function(base_size = 12) {
  theme_bw(base_size = base_size) %+replace%
    theme(
      panel.grid.minor  = element_blank(),
      panel.border      = element_rect(colour = "#cdd8e6"),
      plot.title        = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size + 1,
                                       colour = "#1a1c2e"),
      plot.subtitle     = element_text(colour = "#3d4560", size = base_size - 1),
      legend.position   = "bottom",
      legend.key.size   = unit(0.75, "lines"),
      legend.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
      strip.background  = element_rect(fill = "#e6f1fb"),
      strip.text        = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#0b2d5e"),
      axis.title        = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size - 0.5),
      axis.text         = element_text(colour = "#3d4560")
    )
}

1.2 R: Data Loading & Preparation

The cleaned dataset is loaded from t2dm_clean.csv. Factor levels are encoded explicitly to ensure correct reference categories and ordering throughout all subsequent models. Gender uses Female as the reference category. The dataset is sorted by patient_id and time_num, a requirement for geepack to correctly identify within-cluster observation order.

ImportantBefore Rendering: Set Your Data Path

Update data_path in the chunk below to the full path of your t2dm_clean.csv file if it is not in the same folder as this .qmd file. For example:

data_path <- "C:/Users/User/Documents/MyProject/t2dm_clean.csv"
Code 
# ── ✏️  SET THIS PATH to your t2dm_clean.csv location ────────
# If the CSV is in the same folder as this .qmd, leave as-is.
# Otherwise, provide the full file path, e.g.:
#   data_path <- "C:/Users/User/Documents/t2dm_clean.csv"
data_path <- "t2dm_clean.csv"

df <- read_csv(data_path) |>
  mutate(
    patient_id = factor(patient_id),
    # Numeric time index (0, 1, 2) - required for random slope models
    time_num = as.numeric(factor(time_step,
               levels = c("T1_Pre", "T2_Mid", "T3_Post"))) - 1,

    # Ordered factors - reference category is the first (clinically worst) level
    time_step = factor(time_step,
                  levels = c("T1_Pre", "T2_Mid", "T3_Post")),
    intervention_strength = factor(intervention_strength,
                  levels = c("Weak", "Standard", "Intensive")),
    physical_activity_level = factor(physical_activity_level,
                  levels = c("Low", "Moderate", "High")),
    medication_adherence = factor(medication_adherence,
                  levels = c("Poor", "Moderate", "Good")),
    comorbidity_level = factor(comorbidity_level,
                  levels = c("Low", "Medium", "High")),
    gender = factor(gender, levels = c("Female", "Male"))
  ) |>
  # CRITICAL for GEE: data must be sorted by cluster ID then time
  arrange(patient_id, time_num)

cat(sprintf("Observations : %d\n", nrow(df)))
Observations : 60000
Code 
cat(sprintf("Patients     : %d\n", n_distinct(df$patient_id)))
Patients     : 20000
Code 
cat(sprintf("Time points  : %d per patient (balanced)\n",
            nrow(df) / n_distinct(df$patient_id)))
Time points  : 3 per patient (balanced)
Code 
cat(sprintf("HbA1c range  : %.2f - %.2f %%\n",
            min(df$hba1c), max(df$hba1c)))
HbA1c range  : 4.60 - 13.13 %
Code 
cat("=== Dataset Structure (glimpse) ===\n")
=== Dataset Structure (glimpse) ===
Code 
glimpse(df)
Rows: 60,000
Columns: 14
$ patient_id              <fct> 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 5, 6…
$ time_step               <fct> T1_Pre, T2_Mid, T3_Post, T1_Pre, T2_Mid, T3_Po…
$ age                     <dbl> 73, 73, 73, 72, 72, 72, 68, 68, 68, 51, 51, 51…
$ gender                  <fct> Male, Male, Male, Female, Female, Female, Male…
$ bmi                     <dbl> 32.96, 31.77, 30.94, 23.06, 23.25, 22.46, 36.2…
$ hba1c                   <dbl> 7.12, 5.83, 4.78, 11.30, 11.41, 11.46, 7.76, 7…
$ physical_activity_level <fct> High, High, High, Low, Low, Low, High, High, H…
$ diet_score              <dbl> 8.2, 8.9, 9.8, 7.2, 7.2, 6.9, 3.4, 3.5, 4.2, 5…
$ sleep_hours             <dbl> 4.0, 4.6, 4.6, 6.7, 6.3, 6.1, 7.6, 7.5, 7.6, 7…
$ medication_adherence    <fct> Good, Good, Good, Moderate, Poor, Poor, Poor, …
$ intervention_strength   <fct> Standard, Standard, Standard, Standard, Standa…
$ comorbidity_level       <fct> Medium, Medium, Medium, Medium, Medium, Medium…
$ time_index              <dbl> 0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2, 0…
$ time_num                <dbl> 0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2, 0…
NoteData Structure Confirmation

glimpse() confirms the dataset contains 60,000 rows × 14 variables with no unexpected data types after factor coding. HbA1c is continuous (dbl), time_step and intervention_strength are correctly ordered factors, and patient_id is the unique cluster identifier. The dataset is perfectly balanced with exactly 3 observations per patient, and no missing values are present in any analysis variable.

1.3 E: Exploratory Data Analysis

1.3.1 Data Quality Summary

Three complementary checks confirm data integrity before modelling: skimr::skim() provides a full distributional profile of all variables; colSums(is.na()) counts missing values per column; and DataExplorer::plot_missing() visualises the missingness pattern.

Code 
skimr::skim(df)
Data summary
Name df
Number of rows 60000
Number of columns 14
_______________________
Column type frequency:
factor 7
numeric 7
________________________
Group variables None

Variable type: factor

skim_variable n_missing complete_rate ordered n_unique top_counts
patient_id 0 1 FALSE 20000 1: 3, 2: 3, 3: 3, 4: 3
time_step 0 1 FALSE 3 T1_: 20000, T2_: 20000, T3_: 20000
gender 0 1 FALSE 2 Mal: 30525, Fem: 29475
physical_activity_level 0 1 FALSE 3 Low: 23899, Mod: 18950, Hig: 17151
medication_adherence 0 1 FALSE 3 Goo: 27386, Poo: 16486, Mod: 16128
intervention_strength 0 1 FALSE 3 Sta: 28761, Int: 18297, Wea: 12942
comorbidity_level 0 1 FALSE 3 Med: 26085, Low: 21207, Hig: 12708

Variable type: numeric

skim_variable n_missing complete_rate mean sd p0 p25 p50 p75 p100 hist
age 0 1 53.05 14.71 28.0 40.00 53.00 66.00 78.00 ▇▇▇▇▇
bmi 0 1 31.31 4.87 18.0 28.05 31.28 34.66 47.55 ▁▆▇▃▁
hba1c 0 1 8.34 1.31 4.6 7.48 8.38 9.24 13.13 ▁▆▇▂▁
diet_score 0 1 5.45 1.83 1.0 4.20 5.40 6.70 10.00 ▂▆▇▅▂
sleep_hours 0 1 6.67 1.05 4.0 5.90 6.70 7.40 9.50 ▂▆▇▅▁
time_index 0 1 1.00 0.82 0.0 0.00 1.00 2.00 2.00 ▇▁▇▁▇
time_num 0 1 1.00 0.82 0.0 0.00 1.00 2.00 2.00 ▇▁▇▁▇
Code 
colSums(is.na(df))
             patient_id               time_step                     age 
                      0                       0                       0 
                 gender                     bmi                   hba1c 
                      0                       0                       0 
physical_activity_level              diet_score             sleep_hours 
                      0                       0                       0 
   medication_adherence   intervention_strength       comorbidity_level 
                      0                       0                       0 
             time_index                time_num 
                      0                       0
Code 
DataExplorer::plot_missing(df)

1.3.2 Balance Check

A longitudinal dataset is balanced if every subject contributes the same number of observations at the same time points. This check confirms that each of the 20,000 patients appears exactly three times (once per time point), ruling out phantom rows, duplicate entries, or missing visits that would complicate subsequent analysis.

Code 
df |> count(time_step)                 # expect 20,000 per time point
Code 
df |> count(patient_id) |> summary()  # expect min = max = 3
   patient_id          n    
 1      :    1   Min.   :3  
 2      :    1   1st Qu.:3  
 3      :    1   Median :3  
 4      :    1   Mean   :3  
 5      :    1   3rd Qu.:3  
 6      :    1   Max.   :3  
 (Other):19994

A perfectly balanced panel means no complications from unequal cluster sizes. Both LMM and GEE handle unbalanced data, but confirmed balance rules out data entry issues and ensures the ICCand QIC computations reflect the intended design.

1.3.3 Spaghetti Plot: Individual Trajectories

Code 
set.seed(123)
sample_pts <- sample(levels(df$patient_id), 100)

ggplot(df |> filter(patient_id %in% sample_pts),
       aes(x = time_step, y = hba1c, group = patient_id)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.25, colour = "steelblue") +
  stat_summary(aes(group = 1), fun = mean, geom = "line",
               colour = "red", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "HbA1c Individual Trajectories (n = 100 patients; red = group mean)",
       x = "Time Point", y = "HbA1c (%)") +
  theme_gee()

A descending red mean line confirms HbA1c improves on average over time. The wide spread of individual blue lines demonstrates strong between-patient heterogeneity in baseline HbA1c, quantified later by the ICC. Lines that cross (non-parallel trajectories) suggest patients also differ in their rate of change, motivating the random slope extension in LMM.

1.3.4 Mean Trajectory by Intervention Strength

Code 
df |>
  group_by(time_step, intervention_strength) |>
  summarise(mean_hba1c = mean(hba1c), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = time_step, y = mean_hba1c,
             colour = intervention_strength, group = intervention_strength)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = cb_intervention) +
  labs(title = "Mean HbA1c Over Time by Intervention Strength",
       x = "Time Point", y = "Mean HbA1c (%)",
       colour = "Intervention") +
  theme_gee()

If lines run parallel, there is no interaction, the intervention effect is constant across time points. Diverging lines (Intensive dropping fastest) signal a time × intervention interaction: the benefit of higher-intensity intervention accumulates over time, which will be formally tested in both the LMM and GEE interaction models.

1.3.5 HbA1c Distribution by Group

Code 
df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = time_step, y = hba1c,
             fill = intervention_strength,
             colour = intervention_strength)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.35, trim = TRUE, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.75, width = 0.22, outlier.size = 0.3,
               position = position_dodge(0.9)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = cb_intervention, name = "Intervention Strength") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = cb_intervention, name = "Intervention Strength") +
  labs(
    title    = "HbA1c Distribution by Time Point and Intervention Strength",
    subtitle = "Violin + box plots | 20,000 patients, 3 time points",
    x = "Time Point", y = "HbA1c (%)"
  ) +
  theme_gee()
TipFigure 3 Interpretation

At T1_Pre, all three groups show near-identical median HbA1c (~8.5–8.6%), confirming baseline group comparability. A progressive downward shift is visible across all groups from T1_Pre to T3_Post. By T3_Post, the Intensive group (cobalt blue) has visibly lower median HbA1c than Standard, which in turn is lower than Weak, indicating a dose–response relationship that motivates the formal interaction analysis. The stable interquartile range across time points supports the homoscedasticity assumption for both LMM and GEE.

1.3.6 Correlation Matrix

Code 
cont_vars <- df |>
  dplyr::select(where(is.double))

cor_matrix <- cor(as.matrix(cont_vars), use = "complete.obs")
corrplot::corrplot(cor_matrix, method = "color", type = "upper",
                   addCoef.col = "black", tl.col = "black", tl.srt = 45)

No pairwise correlation exceeds |r| = 0.8, confirming the absence of severe multicollinearity among the available continuous predictors.

1.3.7 Table 1: Baseline Patient Characteristics

Code 
df |>
  filter(time_step == "T1_Pre") |>
  dplyr::select(age, gender, bmi, hba1c,
                physical_activity_level, diet_score, sleep_hours,
                medication_adherence, intervention_strength,
                comorbidity_level) |>
  tbl_summary(
    statistic = list(
      all_continuous()  ~ "{mean} ({sd})",
      all_categorical() ~ "{n} ({p}%)"
    ),
    missing = "no",
    label = list(
      age                     ~ "Age (years)",
      gender                  ~ "Gender",
      hba1c                   ~ "HbA1c (%)",
      bmi                     ~ "BMI (kg/m²)",
      diet_score              ~ "Diet Score",
      sleep_hours             ~ "Sleep Hours",
      physical_activity_level ~ "Physical Activity Level",
      medication_adherence    ~ "Medication Adherence",
      intervention_strength   ~ "Intervention Strength",
      comorbidity_level       ~ "Comorbidity Level"
    )
  ) |>
  bold_labels() |>
  modify_caption("**Table 1. Baseline Characteristics at T1_Pre (n = 20,000)**")
Table 1. Baseline Characteristics at T1_Pre (n = 20,000)
Characteristic N = 20,0001
Age (years) 53 (15)
Gender
    Female 9,825 (49%)
    Male 10,175 (51%)
BMI (kg/m²) 31.6 (4.8)
HbA1c (%) 8.71 (1.10)
Physical Activity Level
    Low 8,022 (40%)
    Moderate 7,624 (38%)
    High 4,354 (22%)
Diet Score 5.20 (1.70)
Sleep Hours 6.60 (1.00)
Medication Adherence
    Poor 4,720 (24%)
    Moderate 6,851 (34%)
    Good 8,429 (42%)
Intervention Strength
    Weak 4,314 (22%)
    Standard 9,587 (48%)
    Intensive 6,099 (30%)
Comorbidity Level
    Low 7,069 (35%)
    Medium 8,695 (43%)
    High 4,236 (21%)
1 Mean (SD); n (%)

Table 1 characterises the study cohort at baseline (T1_Pre only). Filtering to baseline is essential, including all three time points would inflate n to 60,000, misrepresenting the actual cohort size of 20,000 patients. This table establishes the starting point before any intervention effects have occurred.

1.3.8 Table 2: Longitudinal Descriptive Statistics by Time Point

Code 
df |>
  dplyr::select(hba1c, time_step, intervention_strength,
                age, gender, bmi, physical_activity_level,
                diet_score, sleep_hours, medication_adherence,
                comorbidity_level) |>
  tbl_summary(
    by = time_step,
    statistic = list(
      all_continuous()  ~ "{mean} ({sd})",
      all_categorical() ~ "{n} ({p}%)"
    ),
    digits = list(
      all_continuous()  ~ 2,
      all_categorical() ~ c(0, 1)
    ),
    label = list(
      hba1c                   ~ "HbA1c (%)",
      intervention_strength   ~ "Intervention Strength",
      age                     ~ "Age (years)",
      gender                  ~ "Gender",
      bmi                     ~ "BMI (kg/m²)",
      physical_activity_level ~ "Physical Activity Level",
      diet_score              ~ "Diet Score",
      sleep_hours             ~ "Sleep Hours",
      medication_adherence    ~ "Medication Adherence",
      comorbidity_level       ~ "Comorbidity Level"
    ),
    missing = "no"
  ) |>
  add_overall() |>
  bold_labels() |>
  modify_header(label ~ "**Variable**") |>
  modify_caption("**Table 2. Descriptive Characteristics by Time Point (n = 60,000 observations)**")
Table 2. Descriptive Characteristics by Time Point (n = 60,000 observations)
Variable Overall
N = 60,0001		 T1_Pre
N = 20,0001		 T2_Mid
N = 20,0001		 T3_Post
N = 20,0001
HbA1c (%) 8.34 (1.31) 8.71 (1.10) 8.31 (1.25) 8.00 (1.46)
Intervention Strength



    Weak 12,942 (21.6%) 4,314 (21.6%) 4,314 (21.6%) 4,314 (21.6%)
    Standard 28,761 (47.9%) 9,587 (47.9%) 9,587 (47.9%) 9,587 (47.9%)
    Intensive 18,297 (30.5%) 6,099 (30.5%) 6,099 (30.5%) 6,099 (30.5%)
Age (years) 53.05 (14.71) 53.05 (14.71) 53.05 (14.71) 53.05 (14.71)
Gender



    Female 29,475 (49.1%) 9,825 (49.1%) 9,825 (49.1%) 9,825 (49.1%)
    Male 30,525 (50.9%) 10,175 (50.9%) 10,175 (50.9%) 10,175 (50.9%)
BMI (kg/m²) 31.31 (4.87) 31.57 (4.82) 31.31 (4.86) 31.06 (4.92)
Physical Activity Level



    Low 23,899 (39.8%) 8,022 (40.1%) 7,763 (38.8%) 8,114 (40.6%)
    Moderate 18,950 (31.6%) 7,624 (38.1%) 6,035 (30.2%) 5,291 (26.5%)
    High 17,151 (28.6%) 4,354 (21.8%) 6,202 (31.0%) 6,595 (33.0%)
Diet Score 5.45 (1.83) 5.20 (1.70) 5.49 (1.82) 5.67 (1.93)
Sleep Hours 6.67 (1.05) 6.60 (1.00) 6.68 (1.05) 6.74 (1.10)
Medication Adherence



    Poor 16,486 (27.5%) 4,720 (23.6%) 5,573 (27.9%) 6,193 (31.0%)
    Moderate 16,128 (26.9%) 6,851 (34.3%) 4,927 (24.6%) 4,350 (21.8%)
    Good 27,386 (45.6%) 8,429 (42.1%) 9,500 (47.5%) 9,457 (47.3%)
Comorbidity Level



    Low 21,207 (35.3%) 7,069 (35.3%) 7,069 (35.3%) 7,069 (35.3%)
    Medium 26,085 (43.5%) 8,695 (43.5%) 8,695 (43.5%) 8,695 (43.5%)
    High 12,708 (21.2%) 4,236 (21.2%) 4,236 (21.2%) 4,236 (21.2%)
1 Mean (SD); n (%)
TipTable 2 Interpretation

Mean HbA1c declined progressively from 8.71% at baseline to 8.00% post-intervention, suggesting a genuine temporal treatment effect. Patient-level fixed characteristics, gender, comorbidity, and age, remained constant across time points as expected. Time-varying behavioural variables showed favourable trends: physical activity (High category: 21.8% → 33.0%), diet score (5.20 → 5.67), and sleep hours (6.60 → 6.74). Mean BMI was 31.3 kg/m², consistent with an obese T2DM cohort.

2 Statistical Assumptions & Analytical Framework

Before fitting any model, two foundational assumptions must be evaluated: the distributional properties of the outcome, and the degree of within-patient clustering. These checks collectively determine which analytical method is required and whether ordinary regression would be sufficient.

2.1 Normality of the Outcome

Code 
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
qqnorm(df$hba1c, main = "Q-Q Plot: HbA1c")
qqline(df$hba1c, col = "red")
hist(df$hba1c, breaks = 50, main = "Distribution of HbA1c",
     xlab = "HbA1c (%)", col = "steelblue", border = "white")
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

Both panels support approximate normality: the Q-Q plot shows points tightly aligned to the diagonal, and the histogram reveals a symmetric, bell-shaped distribution centred near the grand mean.

Note on sample size: With n = 60,000 observations, the Shapiro-Wilk test is not appropriate, at large n it will always reject H₀ for trivially small, clinically irrelevant deviations from normality. Visual inspection is the appropriate approach. Both LMM and GEE are also robust to mild normality violations at large samples due to the Central Limit Theorem.

2.2 Clustering: Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC)

To determine whether the repeated-measures structure requires specialised modelling, an intercept-only (null) LMM is fitted. The ICC derived from this model quantifies the proportion of total HbA1c variance attributable to stable between-patient differences.

\[\text{ICC} = \frac{\sigma^2_{\text{between}}}{\sigma^2_{\text{between}} + \sigma^2_{\text{within}}}\]

Code 
m_null <- lmer(hba1c ~ 1 + (1 | patient_id), data = df, REML = TRUE)
performance::icc(m_null)

Result: ICC = 0.813. This means 81.3% of the total variance in HbA1c is attributable to stable, persistent differences between patients, only 18.7% represents within-patient change over time. This extremely high ICC confirms that ignoring the repeated-measures structure (i.e., running ordinary MLR on all 60,000 rows) would drastically underestimate standard errors and produce many spurious significant findings. Both LMM and GEE are therefore strongly justified.

2.3 Choosing Between LMM and GEE: Complementary Frameworks

NoteLMM vs GEE: Two Answers to Two Different Questions
Feature LMM GEE
Estimand Subject-specific (individual-level) Population-averaged (marginal)
Research question “How does this patient’s HbA1c change?” “What is the expected HbA1c change for a typical patient?”
Random effects Yes, random intercept ± slope No, correlation handled via working matrix
Interpretation Patient-level trajectory Population-level policy estimate
Suitable for prediction? Yes (individual patients) No (population only)
Correlation structure Implicitly via random effects Explicitly via working matrix (QIC-selected)
Public health relevance Understanding heterogeneity Programme evaluation & policy

In this analysis, both methods are applied. LMM provides subject-specific estimates and quantifies the heterogeneity in patient trajectories. GEE provides population-averaged estimates directly interpretable for national T2DM programme evaluation. The two models are complementary, not competing.

2.4 GEE-Specific Assumptions

NoteGEE Has Minimal Formal Assumptions

Unlike MLR or LMM, GEE does not require normality of the outcome or residuals, nor does it assume a distribution for random effects. The key assumptions for valid GEE inference are:

  1. Correct mean model specification: the linear predictor correctly captures the relationship between predictors and HbA1c. Verified via Pearson residual plots in Section 5.
  2. Independence between clusters: HbA1c measurements from different patients are independent. Satisfied by study design.
  3. Constant variance (Gaussian family): appropriate for continuous HbA1c. Supported by stable residual spread in Section 5.
  4. Large sample size: the sandwich variance estimator is asymptotically valid. Satisfied: n = 20,000 patients.
  5. MCAR (if missing data present): GEE requires data to be Missing Completely At Random. No missing values in this dataset.

There is no assumption about the correctness of the working correlation structure; the sandwich estimator protects inference regardless of structure choice.

3 Linear Mixed Effects Model Analysis

The LMM extends standard linear regression by adding random effects, parameters that vary across higher-level grouping units (patients). This correctly partitions within-patient change from stable between-patient differences, as warranted by the ICC = 0.813 established in Section 3.

The general LMM formula fitted here is:

\[\text{HbA1c}_{ij} = \underbrace{(\beta_0 + u_{0i})}_{\text{patient-specific intercept}} + \beta_1 \cdot \text{time}_{ij} + \beta_2 \cdot \mathbf{X}_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij}\]

Where:

  • \(\beta_0\) = population-average HbA1c intercept
  • \(u_{0i} \sim N(0, \sigma^2_{\text{between}})\) = patient-specific random intercept deviation
  • \(\beta_1, \beta_2\) = fixed effects for time and covariates
  • \(\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2_{\text{within}})\) = within-patient residual

A model-building strategy is employed: four progressively richer models are fitted and compared using likelihood ratio tests and AIC. This approach isolates the contribution of each component and justifies the final model choice on statistical grounds.

3.1 A: Model Building

3.1.1 Null Model (Variance Decomposition)

The null model contains no predictors, only an overall intercept and a random intercept per patient. Its purpose is to decompose total HbA1c variance into between-patient and within-patient components, establishing the baseline ICC.

Code 
lmm_null <- lmer(hba1c ~ 1 + (1 | patient_id), data = df, REML = TRUE)
summary(lmm_null)
Linear mixed model fit by REML. t-tests use Satterthwaite's method [
lmerModLmerTest]
Formula: hba1c ~ 1 + (1 | patient_id)
   Data: df

REML criterion at convergence: 154668.8

Scaled residuals: 
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-2.98997 -0.31341  0.00276  0.32613  2.88830 

Random effects:
 Groups     Name        Variance Std.Dev.
 patient_id (Intercept) 1.3888   1.1785  
 Residual               0.3196   0.5653  
Number of obs: 60000, groups:  patient_id, 20000

Fixed effects:
             Estimate Std. Error        df t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 8.342e+00  8.647e-03 2.000e+04   964.8   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
Code 
performance::icc(lmm_null)

3.1.2 Time-Only Model (Unconditional Growth)

The time-only model adds time_step as the sole fixed-effect predictor. This addresses a foundational question: does HbA1c change significantly over time at all? The intercept estimates mean HbA1c at T1_Pre; coefficients for T2_Mid and T3_Post represent the change from baseline averaged across all patients.

Code 
lmm_time <- lmer(hba1c ~ time_step + (1 | patient_id),
                 data = df, REML = TRUE)
summary(lmm_time)
Linear mixed model fit by REML. t-tests use Satterthwaite's method [
lmerModLmerTest]
Formula: hba1c ~ time_step + (1 | patient_id)
   Data: df

REML criterion at convergence: 134803.6

Scaled residuals: 
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-2.94984 -0.43222  0.00097  0.41861  2.82396 

Random effects:
 Groups     Name        Variance Std.Dev.
 patient_id (Intercept) 1.4305   1.1960  
 Residual               0.1944   0.4409  
Number of obs: 60000, groups:  patient_id, 20000

Fixed effects:
                   Estimate Std. Error         df t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)       8.710e+00  9.014e-03  2.353e+04  966.37   <2e-16 ***
time_stepT2_Mid  -3.993e-01  4.409e-03  4.000e+04  -90.55   <2e-16 ***
time_stepT3_Post -7.056e-01  4.409e-03  4.000e+04 -160.02   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Correlation of Fixed Effects:
            (Intr) t_T2_M
tm_stpT2_Md -0.245       
tm_stpT3_Ps -0.245  0.500

Result: Both T2_Mid (−0.40%) and T3_Post (−0.71%) show highly significant declines from baseline (both p < 0.0001), confirming a meaningful time trend before accounting for any covariates.

3.1.3 Full Fixed-Effects Model

This is the main adjusted model, it adds all available covariates alongside time, providing simultaneous estimates of each predictor’s independent association with HbA1c after controlling for all others. lme4::isSingular() verifies the model has not been over-parameterised.

Code 
lmm_full <- lmer(
  hba1c ~ time_step + age + gender + bmi +
    physical_activity_level + diet_score + sleep_hours +
    medication_adherence + intervention_strength + comorbidity_level +
    (1 | patient_id),
  data = df, REML = TRUE
)

summary(lmm_full)
Linear mixed model fit by REML. t-tests use Satterthwaite's method [
lmerModLmerTest]
Formula: hba1c ~ time_step + age + gender + bmi + physical_activity_level +  
    diet_score + sleep_hours + medication_adherence + intervention_strength +  
    comorbidity_level + (1 | patient_id)
   Data: df

REML criterion at convergence: 102015.8

Scaled residuals: 
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-3.7033 -0.4949 -0.0030  0.4896  4.0874 

Random effects:
 Groups     Name        Variance Std.Dev.
 patient_id (Intercept) 1.80592  1.3438  
 Residual               0.07721  0.2779  
Number of obs: 60000, groups:  patient_id, 20000

Fixed effects:
                                  Estimate Std. Error         df  t value
(Intercept)                      1.066e+01  8.203e-02  2.551e+04  129.977
time_stepT2_Mid                 -2.155e-01  2.974e-03  3.769e+04  -72.483
time_stepT3_Post                -4.205e-01  3.249e-03  4.120e+04 -129.409
age                              1.313e-03  6.505e-04  1.624e+04    2.018
genderMale                      -1.707e-02  1.915e-02  1.624e+04   -0.891
bmi                              8.687e-02  1.780e-03  2.482e+04   48.795
physical_activity_levelModerate -1.945e-01  6.469e-03  4.417e+04  -30.067
physical_activity_levelHigh     -5.155e-01  9.173e-03  4.872e+04  -56.199
diet_score                      -3.205e-01  3.072e-03  5.996e+04 -104.352
sleep_hours                     -4.059e-01  4.818e-03  5.919e+04  -84.234
medication_adherenceModerate    -1.752e-01  7.706e-03  4.499e+04  -22.737
medication_adherenceGood        -3.554e-01  1.010e-02  5.135e+04  -35.177
intervention_strengthStandard   -2.819e-02  2.482e-02  1.625e+04   -1.135
intervention_strengthIntensive  -7.660e-02  2.696e-02  1.630e+04   -2.842
comorbidity_levelMedium          2.386e-02  2.168e-02  1.625e+04    1.100
comorbidity_levelHigh            5.749e-02  2.632e-02  1.628e+04    2.184
                                Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)                      < 2e-16 ***
time_stepT2_Mid                  < 2e-16 ***
time_stepT3_Post                 < 2e-16 ***
age                              0.04364 *  
genderMale                       0.37269    
bmi                              < 2e-16 ***
physical_activity_levelModerate  < 2e-16 ***
physical_activity_levelHigh      < 2e-16 ***
diet_score                       < 2e-16 ***
sleep_hours                      < 2e-16 ***
medication_adherenceModerate     < 2e-16 ***
medication_adherenceGood         < 2e-16 ***
intervention_strengthStandard    0.25618    
intervention_strengthIntensive   0.00449 ** 
comorbidity_levelMedium          0.27117    
comorbidity_levelHigh            0.02895 *  
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
Code 
lme4::isSingular(lmm_full)   # check for over-parameterisation
[1] FALSE

Result: isSingular() = FALSE, the model converged cleanly. All fixed-effect predictors except gender (Male) and intervention (Standard) are statistically significant.

3.1.4 Random Slope Extension

The random intercept model assumes all patients share the same average rate of change over time. The random slope model relaxes this: (1 + time_num | patient_id) allows both the intercept (starting level) and the slope (rate of change) to vary across patients.

Code 
lmm_slope <- lmer(
  hba1c ~ time_step + age + gender + bmi +
    physical_activity_level + diet_score + sleep_hours +
    medication_adherence + intervention_strength + comorbidity_level +
    (1 + time_num | patient_id),
  data = df, REML = TRUE
)

lme4::isSingular(lmm_slope)
[1] FALSE

Result: isSingular() = FALSE, the random slope model is supported by the data, confirming that patients differ not only in their baseline HbA1c but also in their rate of change over time. However, because the primary clinical question concerns group-level trajectories, the interaction model below is the more relevant final model.

3.1.5 Interaction Model: Time × Intervention

This model directly addresses the primary research question by testing whether intervention strength groups follow different HbA1c trajectories over time. The * operator creates both main effects and all pairwise interaction terms:

time_step * intervention_strength expands to: time_step + intervention_strength + time_step:intervention_strength

ImportantCritical Interpretation Rule

When a statistically significant interaction is present, main effects must not be interpreted in isolation. The main effect of time_step describes only the Weak group (reference); the main effect of intervention_strength describes only T1_Pre (reference). Always use emmeans() to obtain properly marginalised cell-level estimates when an interaction is present.

Code 
lmm_int <- lmer(
  hba1c ~ time_step * intervention_strength +
    age + gender + bmi + physical_activity_level + diet_score +
    sleep_hours + medication_adherence + comorbidity_level +
    (1 | patient_id),
  data = df, REML = TRUE
)

summary(lmm_int)
Linear mixed model fit by REML. t-tests use Satterthwaite's method [
lmerModLmerTest]
Formula: hba1c ~ time_step * intervention_strength + age + gender + bmi +  
    physical_activity_level + diet_score + sleep_hours + medication_adherence +  
    comorbidity_level + (1 | patient_id)
   Data: df

REML criterion at convergence: 101240.2

Scaled residuals: 
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-3.9055 -0.4954 -0.0013  0.4882  4.1503 

Random effects:
 Groups     Name        Variance Std.Dev.
 patient_id (Intercept) 1.77086  1.3307  
 Residual               0.07641  0.2764  
Number of obs: 60000, groups:  patient_id, 20000

Fixed effects:
                                                  Estimate Std. Error
(Intercept)                                      1.055e+01  8.149e-02
time_stepT2_Mid                                 -1.698e-01  6.011e-03
time_stepT3_Post                                -3.159e-01  6.110e-03
intervention_strengthStandard                    1.209e-02  2.492e-02
intervention_strengthIntensive                   2.158e-02  2.704e-02

genderMale                                      -1.696e-02  1.896e-02
bmi                                              8.443e-02  1.766e-03

medication_adherenceGood                        -3.221e-01  1.013e-02

comorbidity_levelHigh                            6.511e-02  2.607e-02

time_stepT3_Post:intervention_strengthStandard  -9.225e-02  7.237e-03

time_stepT3_Post:intervention_strengthIntensive -2.235e-01  8.074e-03
                                                        df  t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)                                      2.584e+04  129.434  < 2e-16
time_stepT2_Mid                                  3.622e+04  -28.246  < 2e-16
time_stepT3_Post                                 3.686e+04  -51.704  < 2e-16
intervention_strengthStandard                    1.733e+04    0.485   0.6277
intervention_strengthIntensive                   1.734e+04    0.798   0.4249
age                                              1.631e+04    2.053   0.0401
genderMale                                       1.631e+04   -0.894   0.3711
bmi                                              2.479e+04   47.810  < 2e-16
physical_activity_levelModerate                  4.436e+04  -28.463  < 2e-16
physical_activity_levelHigh                      4.887e+04  -53.925  < 2e-16
diet_score                                       5.994e+04 -101.836  < 2e-16
sleep_hours                                      5.922e+04  -82.441  < 2e-16
medication_adherenceModerate                     4.525e+04  -21.052  < 2e-16
medication_adherenceGood                         5.179e+04  -31.800  < 2e-16
comorbidity_levelMedium                          1.633e+04    1.280   0.2007
comorbidity_levelHigh                            1.636e+04    2.498   0.0125
time_stepT2_Mid:intervention_strengthStandard    3.607e+04   -5.956 2.61e-09
time_stepT3_Post:intervention_strengthStandard   3.624e+04  -12.747  < 2e-16
time_stepT2_Mid:intervention_strengthIntensive   3.646e+04  -12.739  < 2e-16
time_stepT3_Post:intervention_strengthIntensive  3.708e+04  -27.682  < 2e-16
                                                   
(Intercept)                                     ***
time_stepT2_Mid                                 ***
time_stepT3_Post                                ***
intervention_strengthStandard                      
intervention_strengthIntensive                     
age                                             *  
genderMale                                         
bmi                                             ***
physical_activity_levelModerate                 ***
physical_activity_levelHigh                     ***
diet_score                                      ***
sleep_hours                                     ***
medication_adherenceModerate                    ***
medication_adherenceGood                        ***
comorbidity_levelMedium                            
comorbidity_levelHigh                           *  
time_stepT2_Mid:intervention_strengthStandard   ***
time_stepT3_Post:intervention_strengthStandard  ***
time_stepT2_Mid:intervention_strengthIntensive  ***
time_stepT3_Post:intervention_strengthIntensive ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Result: All four interaction terms are highly significant (all p < 0.0001), confirming that intervention groups diverge in their HbA1c trajectories over time. The Intensive group shows the steepest decline.

3.2 A: Model Comparison

Formal model comparison determines which of the four models best fits the data. Two complementary approaches are used:

  1. Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT) via anova(): valid for nested models.
  2. AIC (Akaike Information Criterion): valid for both nested and non-nested models; lower AIC = better balance of fit and parsimony; a difference > 10 is considered strong evidence.

Why refit with REML = FALSE? REML cannot validly compare models with different fixed-effect structures. ML must be used for fixed-effects comparisons; REML is used only for final parameter estimates.

Code 
lmm_null_ml  <- update(lmm_null,  REML = FALSE)
lmm_time_ml  <- update(lmm_time,  REML = FALSE)
lmm_full_ml  <- update(lmm_full,  REML = FALSE)
lmm_int_ml   <- update(lmm_int,   REML = FALSE)

anova(lmm_null_ml, lmm_time_ml, lmm_full_ml)
Code 
AIC(lmm_null_ml, lmm_time_ml, lmm_full_ml, lmm_int_ml)
Model AIC Improvement
Null 154,667 Baseline, no predictors
Time-only 134,788 −19,879; time highly significant
Full covariates 101,920 −32,868; covariates essential
Interaction 101,119 −801; interaction significant

The interaction model achieves the lowest AIC and is confirmed as the best-fitting model. Each step’s improvement is statistically significant (all p < 2.2×10⁻¹⁶ by LRT).

3.3 A: Model Assessment

3.3.1 Variance Explained: Marginal and Conditional R²

LMMs have two R² values (Nakagawa & Schielzeth, 2013):

  • R²m (marginal) = proportion of total variance explained by fixed effects only
  • R²c (conditional) = proportion explained by fixed + random effects combined

A large gap between R²m and R²c confirms the random intercept is absorbing substantial between-patient variance, validating the LMM approach.

Code 
MuMIn::r.squaredGLMM(lmm_full)
           R2m       R2c
[1,] 0.3677706 0.9740781
Code 
performance::r2(lmm_full)
# R2 for Mixed Models

  Conditional R2: 0.974
     Marginal R2: 0.368
Code 
performance::icc(lmm_full)

Results: R²m = 0.368, R²c = 0.974. Fixed effects alone explain 36.8% of variance; once patient-level random intercepts are included, 97.4% is explained. The gap (~60%) represents stable between-patient differences that the measured covariates cannot fully capture, confirming LMM is strongly justified.

3.3.2 Residual Diagnostics

Code 
plot(lmm_full)                                    # residuals vs fitted
Code 
qqnorm(resid(lmm_full)); qqline(resid(lmm_full))  # normality of residuals

Both panels confirm adequate model specification: no systematic pattern in the residual plot and points closely aligned to the Q-Q diagonal.

3.4 A: Results: Estimated Marginal Means & Contrasts

3.4.1 Within-Group Time Contrasts

When a significant interaction is present, emmeans() calculates model-predicted mean HbA1c at each cell of the time × intervention grid, marginalised over all other covariates. contrast() then performs all pairwise comparisons within each intervention group across time, with Bonferroni correction.

Code 
emm_int <- emmeans::emmeans(lmm_int, ~ time_step | intervention_strength)
emm_int
intervention_strength = Weak:
 time_step emmean     SE  df asymp.LCL asymp.UCL
 T1_Pre     8.554 0.0210 Inf     8.513     8.595
 T2_Mid     8.384 0.0209 Inf     8.343     8.425
 T3_Post    8.238 0.0209 Inf     8.197     8.279

intervention_strength = Standard:
 time_step emmean     SE  df asymp.LCL asymp.UCL
 T1_Pre     8.566 0.0143 Inf     8.538     8.594
 T2_Mid     8.354 0.0142 Inf     8.326     8.381
 T3_Post    8.158 0.0142 Inf     8.130     8.186

intervention_strength = Intensive:
 time_step emmean     SE  df asymp.LCL asymp.UCL
 T1_Pre     8.576 0.0177 Inf     8.541     8.610
 T2_Mid     8.305 0.0177 Inf     8.270     8.340
 T3_Post    8.036 0.0178 Inf     8.001     8.071

Results are averaged over the levels of: gender, physical_activity_level, medication_adherence, comorbidity_level 
Degrees-of-freedom method: asymptotic 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Code 
emmeans::contrast(emm_int, method = "pairwise", adjust = "bonferroni")
intervention_strength = Weak:
 contrast         estimate      SE  df z.ratio p.value
 T1_Pre - T2_Mid     0.170 0.00601 Inf  28.246 <0.0001
 T1_Pre - T3_Post    0.316 0.00611 Inf  51.704 <0.0001
 T2_Mid - T3_Post    0.146 0.00598 Inf  24.443 <0.0001

intervention_strength = Standard:
 contrast         estimate      SE  df z.ratio p.value
 T1_Pre - T2_Mid     0.213 0.00413 Inf  51.546 <0.0001
 T1_Pre - T3_Post    0.408 0.00431 Inf  94.614 <0.0001
 T2_Mid - T3_Post    0.196 0.00405 Inf  48.259 <0.0001

intervention_strength = Intensive:
 contrast         estimate      SE  df z.ratio p.value
 T1_Pre - T2_Mid     0.271 0.00525 Inf  51.570 <0.0001
 T1_Pre - T3_Post    0.539 0.00556 Inf  97.003 <0.0001
 T2_Mid - T3_Post    0.269 0.00510 Inf  52.742 <0.0001

Results are averaged over the levels of: gender, physical_activity_level, medication_adherence, comorbidity_level 
Degrees-of-freedom method: asymptotic 
P value adjustment: bonferroni method for 3 tests
Code 
emmeans::emmip(lmm_int, intervention_strength ~ time_step, CIs = TRUE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Weak"      = "#e74c3c",
                                 "Standard"  = "#f39c12",
                                 "Intensive" = "#27ae60")) +
  labs(
    title    = "LMM: HbA1c Trajectories by Intervention Strength",
    subtitle = "Estimated marginal means (95% CI shaded) - adjusted for all covariates",
    x        = "Time Point",
    y        = "HbA1c (%) - Estimated Marginal Mean",
    colour   = "Intervention Strength"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12)

3.4.2 Between-Group Contrasts at Endpoint (T3_Post)

This block answers the complementary question: At the study endpoint, do the three intervention groups differ significantly from each other? Bonferroni correction is applied across the three pairwise contrasts.

Code 
emm_t3 <- emmeans::emmeans(lmm_int, ~ intervention_strength | time_step,
                            at = list(time_step = "T3_Post"))
emm_t3
time_step = T3_Post:
 intervention_strength emmean     SE  df asymp.LCL asymp.UCL
 Weak                   8.238 0.0209 Inf     8.197     8.279
 Standard               8.158 0.0142 Inf     8.130     8.186
 Intensive              8.036 0.0178 Inf     8.001     8.071

Results are averaged over the levels of: gender, physical_activity_level, medication_adherence, comorbidity_level 
Degrees-of-freedom method: asymptotic 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Code 
emmeans::contrast(emm_t3, method = "pairwise", adjust = "bonferroni")
time_step = T3_Post:
 contrast             estimate     SE  df z.ratio p.value
 Weak - Standard        0.0802 0.0249 Inf   3.214  0.0039
 Weak - Intensive       0.2019 0.0271 Inf   7.442 <0.0001
 Standard - Intensive   0.1218 0.0223 Inf   5.462 <0.0001

Results are averaged over the levels of: gender, physical_activity_level, medication_adherence, comorbidity_level 
Degrees-of-freedom method: asymptotic 
P value adjustment: bonferroni method for 3 tests

At T3_Post, the estimated marginal HbA1c means are:

  • Weak: 8.238% (95% CI: 8.197–8.279)
  • Standard: 8.158% (95% CI: 8.130–8.186)
  • Intensive: 8.036% (95% CI: 8.001–8.071)

All three pairwise comparisons are statistically significant after Bonferroni correction. Intensive intervention achieves HbA1c 0.202% lower than Weak (p < 0.0001) and 0.122% lower than Standard (p < 0.0001). Even Standard confers a significant advantage over Weak (p = 0.0039), confirming a dose–response gradient across all three tiers at the study endpoint.

3.5 P: Final Results: Model Table & Forest Plot

3.5.1 Final LMM Model Table

The table below is the primary results table for the LMM analysis, reporting fixed-effect estimates from the best-fitting model (lmm_int) with 95% confidence intervals, p-values, random-effect variance components, ICC, R²m, R²c, and AIC.

Code 
sjPlot::tab_model(
  lmm_int,
  show.re.var = TRUE,
  show.icc    = TRUE,
  show.r2     = TRUE,
  show.aic    = TRUE,
  pred.labels = c(
    "(Intercept)",
    "Time: T2_Mid",
    "Time: T3_Post",
    "Intervention: Standard",
    "Intervention: Intensive",
    "Age",
    "Gender: Male",
    "BMI",
    "Physical Activity: Moderate",
    "Physical Activity: High",
    "Diet Score",
    "Sleep Hours",
    "Medication Adherence: Moderate",
    "Medication Adherence: Good",
    "Comorbidity: Medium",
    "Comorbidity: High",
    "T2_Mid × Standard",
    "T3_Post × Standard",
    "T2_Mid × Intensive",
    "T3_Post × Intensive"
  ),
  title = "Table 3: LMM Interaction Model - Predictors of HbA1c (n = 60,000 observations; 20,000 patients)"
)
Table 3: LMM Interaction Model - Predictors of HbA1c (n = 60,000 observations; 20,000 patients)
  hba 1 c
Predictors Estimates CI p
(Intercept) 10.55 10.39 – 10.71 <0.001
Time: T2_Mid -0.17 -0.18 – -0.16 <0.001
Time: T3_Post -0.32 -0.33 – -0.30 <0.001
Intervention: Standard 0.01 -0.04 – 0.06 0.628
Intervention: Intensive 0.02 -0.03 – 0.07 0.425
Age 0.00 0.00 – 0.00 0.040
Gender: Male -0.02 -0.05 – 0.02 0.371
BMI 0.08 0.08 – 0.09 <0.001
Physical Activity: Moderate -0.18 -0.20 – -0.17 <0.001
Physical Activity: High -0.49 -0.51 – -0.48 <0.001
Diet Score -0.31 -0.32 – -0.31 <0.001
Sleep Hours -0.40 -0.41 – -0.39 <0.001
Medication Adherence: Moderate -0.16 -0.18 – -0.15 <0.001
Medication Adherence: Good -0.32 -0.34 – -0.30 <0.001
Comorbidity: Medium 0.03 -0.01 – 0.07 0.201
Comorbidity: High 0.07 0.01 – 0.12 0.012
T2_Mid × Standard -0.04 -0.06 – -0.03 <0.001
T3_Post × Standard -0.09 -0.11 – -0.08 <0.001
T2_Mid × Intensive -0.10 -0.12 – -0.09 <0.001
T3_Post × Intensive -0.22 -0.24 – -0.21 <0.001
Random Effects
σ2 0.08
τ00 patient_id 1.77
ICC 0.96
N patient_id 20000
Observations 60000
Marginal R2 / Conditional R2 0.360 / 0.974
AIC 101284.157

3.5.2 Forest Plot: Fixed Effects

Code 
sjPlot::plot_model(
  lmm_full,
  type         = "est",
  show.values  = TRUE,
  show.p       = TRUE,
  value.offset = 0.35,
  title = "Fixed-Effect Estimates from LMM (lmm_full)\nReference: T1_Pre, Female, Low Activity, Poor Adherence, Weak Intervention, Low Comorbidity"
) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40")

3.5.3 Model Fit Summary

Code 
r2_lmm  <- MuMIn::r.squaredGLMM(lmm_full)
lmm_r2m <- r2_lmm[1, "R2m"]
lmm_r2c <- r2_lmm[1, "R2c"]
icc_val <- as.numeric(performance::icc(lmm_null)$ICC_adjusted)
Metric Value Interpretation
ICC (null model) 0.813 81.3% of HbA1c variance is between patients: LMM required
R²m (marginal) 0.368 Fixed effects explain 36.8% of total variance
R²c (conditional) 0.974 Fixed + random effects explain 97.4% of total variance
AIC (lmm_int) 101,119 Best-fitting model across all four candidates

3.6 LMM Conclusions

This analysis examined longitudinal HbA1c trajectories in 20,000 patients across three time points using LMM. The null model ICC of 0.813 confirmed that 81.3% of total HbA1c variance is attributable to stable between-patient differences, validating the LMM framework.

All four time × intervention interaction terms were statistically significant (all p < 0.0001). The Intensive intervention produced the steepest HbA1c decline (mean 8.04% at T3_Post vs 8.24% for Weak). The key independent modifiable predictors were: physical activity High (−0.52%), sleep hours (−0.41%), medication adherence Good (−0.36%), and diet score (−0.32%). BMI (+0.09%***) was the most important detrimental factor. At T3_Post, all three intervention groups differed significantly from each other after Bonferroni correction.

4 Generalised Estimating Equations Analysis

Whereas LMM estimates subject-specific effects, GEE estimates population-averaged (marginal) effects, the expected HbA1c change for a typical patient drawn from the T2DM population. These coefficients directly answer policy-relevant questions: “If all Malaysian T2DM patients received Intensive versus Weak intervention, what is the expected population-level HbA1c difference?” The GEE estimand is therefore the appropriate primary method for public health programme evaluation.

4.1 GEE Theoretical Framework

4.1.1 The Marginal (Population-Averaged) Model

GEE estimates the population-averaged expectation of HbA1c. For a continuous outcome with an identity link:

\[ \begin{aligned} E(Y_{ij} \mid \mathbf{X}_{ij}) = \; &\beta_0 + \beta_1 \, \text{T2 Mid}_{ij} + \beta_2 \, \text{T3 Post}_{ij} \\ &+ \beta_3 \, \text{Standard}_{ij} + \beta_4 \, \text{Intensive}_{ij} \\ &+ \beta_5 \, \text{Age}_{ij} + \beta_6 \, \text{Male}_{ij} + \beta_7 \, \text{BMI}_{ij} \\ &+ \beta_8 \, \text{PA Moderate}_{ij} + \beta_9 \, \text{PA High}_{ij} \\ &+ \beta_{10} \, \text{Diet Score}_{ij} + \beta_{11} \, \text{Sleep Hours}_{ij} \\ &+ \beta_{12} \, \text{MedAdh Moderate}_{ij} + \beta_{13} \, \text{MedAdh Good}_{ij} \\ &+ \beta_{14} \, \text{Comorbidity Medium}_{ij} + \beta_{15} \, \text{Comorbidity High}_{ij} \end{aligned} \]

Each \(\beta\) coefficient represents the expected change in HbA1c (%) across the entire T2DM population for a one-unit change in the predictor, holding all other variables constant.

4.1.2 Working Correlation Structures

Four structures were fitted and compared using QIC. The working correlation matrix \(\mathbf{R}_i(\alpha)\) defines the assumed within-patient correlation pattern:

Structure corstr = Assumption Role
Independence "independence" All off-diagonal = 0 Baseline reference
Exchangeable "exchangeable" All pairs = \(\alpha\) Primary candidate
AR(1) "ar1" Correlation decays: \(\alpha^{|j-k|}\) Sensitivity check
Unstructured "unstructured" Each pair has its own \(\alpha_{jk}\) Flexible benchmark

4.1.3 QIC Model Selection Criterion

The Quasi-likelihood under Independence Criterion (Pan, 2001) is the GEE analogue of AIC. Lower QIC indicates a better-fitting correlation structure.

\[\text{QIC} = -2\,\hat{Q}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) + 2\,\text{tr}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\Omega}}_I \hat{\mathbf{V}}_R)\]

The first term (\(-2\hat{Q}\)) measures goodness of fit; the second term (\(2\text{tr}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\Omega}}_I \hat{\mathbf{V}}_R)\)) penalises for complexity. A more complex structure (e.g., Unstructured with 3 free \(\alpha\) parameters) must improve fit substantially to overcome this penalty.

4.1.4 Robustness: The Sandwich Variance Estimator

A critical property of GEE is that valid estimates and correct standard errors are obtained even if the working correlation structure is misspecified, provided the robust sandwich variance estimator is used (Liang & Zeger, 1986). This means β estimates remain consistent and standard errors are corrected using observed residuals, not model assumptions. QIC guides the best-fitting structure, but inference is protected even if the chosen structure is imperfect.

4.2 A: Univariate GEE Screening

Univariate GEE with Exchangeable working correlation screens each predictor individually. Variables with p < 0.25 are retained for multivariable modelling.

Code 
library(purrr)

predictors <- c(
  "time_step", "intervention_strength",
  "age", "gender", "bmi",
  "physical_activity_level", "diet_score",
  "sleep_hours", "medication_adherence",
  "comorbidity_level"
)

uni_list <- map(predictors, function(pred) {
  fml <- as.formula(paste("hba1c ~", pred))
  geeglm(
    fml,
    id     = patient_id,
    data   = df,
    family = gaussian(link = "identity"),
    corstr = "exchangeable"
  )
})

names(uni_list) <- predictors

uni_results <- map_dfr(names(uni_list), function(pred) {
  broom::tidy(uni_list[[pred]], conf.int = TRUE) |>
    filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
    mutate(predictor = pred)
}) |>
  mutate(
    Predictor = case_match(term,
      "time_stepT2_Mid"                      ~ "Time: T2_Mid vs T1_Pre",
      "time_stepT3_Post"                     ~ "Time: T3_Post vs T1_Pre",
      "intervention_strengthStandard"        ~ "Intervention: Standard vs Weak",
      "intervention_strengthIntensive"       ~ "Intervention: Intensive vs Weak",
      "age"                                  ~ "Age (years)",
      "genderMale"                           ~ "Gender: Male vs Female",
      "bmi"                                  ~ "BMI (kg/m²)",
      "physical_activity_levelModerate"      ~ "Physical Activity: Moderate vs Low",
      "physical_activity_levelHigh"          ~ "Physical Activity: High vs Low",
      "diet_score"                           ~ "Diet Score",
      "sleep_hours"                          ~ "Sleep Hours",
      "medication_adherenceModerate"         ~ "Medication Adherence: Moderate vs Poor",
      "medication_adherenceGood"             ~ "Medication Adherence: Good vs Poor",
      "comorbidity_levelMedium"              ~ "Comorbidity: Medium vs Low",
      "comorbidity_levelHigh"                ~ "Comorbidity: High vs Low",
      .default                               = term
    ),
    `Crude β (95% CI)` = sprintf("%.3f (%.3f, %.3f)",
                                  estimate, conf.low, conf.high),
    `p-value` = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "< 0.001***",
      p.value < 0.01  ~ sprintf("%.3f**", p.value),
      p.value < 0.05  ~ sprintf("%.3f*",  p.value),
      TRUE            ~ sprintf("%.3f",    p.value)
    ),
    Significant = ifelse(p.value < 0.05, "✓", "")
  ) |>
  select(Predictor, `Crude β (95% CI)`,
         `Robust SE` = std.error, `p-value`, Significant)
Code 
knitr::kable(
  uni_results,
  digits  = 3,
  caption = "Table 4. Univariate GEE Associations with HbA1c (%) (Exchangeable Working Correlation)"
) |>
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width        = FALSE
  ) |>
  pack_rows("Time Effects",          1, 2)  |>
  pack_rows("Intervention Effects",  3, 4)  |>
  pack_rows("Socio-demographic",     5, 7)  |>
  pack_rows("Lifestyle Factors",     8, 11) |>
  pack_rows("Clinical Factors",     12, 15) |>
  column_spec(3, bold = TRUE, color = "#009E73") |>
  footnote(general = c(
    "Crude β = unadjusted population-averaged change in HbA1c (%) per unit change in predictor.",
    "Each row is from a separate GEE model with only that predictor (simple GEE).",
    "Exchangeable working correlation; robust sandwich variance estimator.",
    "Reference categories: time_step = T1_Pre; intervention = Weak; gender = Female;",
    "  physical_activity = Low; medication_adherence = Poor; comorbidity = Low.",
    "✓ = significant at p < 0.05.",
    "* p < 0.05   ** p < 0.01   *** p < 0.001"
  ))
Table 4. Univariate GEE Associations with HbA1c (%) (Exchangeable Working Correlation)
Predictor Crude β (95% CI) Robust SE p-value Significant
Time Effects
Time: T2_Mid vs T1_Pre -0.399 (-0.406, -0.392) 0.004 < 0.001*** ✓
Time: T3_Post vs T1_Pre -0.706 (-0.718, -0.694) 0.006 < 0.001*** ✓
Intervention Effects
Intervention: Standard vs Weak -0.150 (-0.193, -0.107) 0.022 < 0.001*** ✓
Intervention: Intensive vs Weak -0.350 (-0.397, -0.304) 0.024 < 0.001*** ✓
Socio-demographic
Age (years) 0.002 (0.000, 0.003) 0.001 0.007** ✓
Gender: Male vs Female -0.002 (-0.036, 0.032) 0.017 0.918
BMI (kg/m²) 0.118 (0.114, 0.122) 0.002 < 0.001*** ✓
Lifestyle Factors
Physical Activity: Moderate vs Low -0.325 (-0.345, -0.306) 0.010 < 0.001*** ✓
Physical Activity: High vs Low -1.279 (-1.307, -1.251) 0.014 < 0.001*** ✓
Diet Score -0.641 (-0.649, -0.632) 0.004 < 0.001*** ✓
Sleep Hours -0.732 (-0.747, -0.716) 0.008 < 0.001*** ✓
Clinical Factors
Medication Adherence: Moderate vs Poor -0.085 (-0.101, -0.069) 0.008 < 0.001*** ✓
Medication Adherence: Good vs Poor -0.780 (-0.806, -0.754) 0.013 < 0.001*** ✓
Comorbidity: Medium vs Low 0.124 (0.085, 0.163) 0.020 < 0.001*** ✓
Comorbidity: High vs Low 0.270 (0.224, 0.315) 0.023 < 0.001*** ✓
Note:
Crude β = unadjusted population-averaged change in HbA1c (%) per unit change in predictor.
Each row is from a separate GEE model with only that predictor (simple GEE).
Exchangeable working correlation; robust sandwich variance estimator.
Reference categories: time_step = T1_Pre; intervention = Weak; gender = Female;
physical_activity = Low; medication_adherence = Poor; comorbidity = Low.
✓ = significant at p < 0.05.
* p < 0.05 ** p < 0.01 *** p < 0.001
Code 
uni_plot <- map_dfr(names(uni_list), function(pred) {
  broom::tidy(uni_list[[pred]], conf.int = TRUE) |>
    filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
    mutate(predictor = pred)
}) |>
  mutate(
    label = case_match(term,
      "time_stepT2_Mid"                      ~ "Time: T2_Mid",
      "time_stepT3_Post"                     ~ "Time: T3_Post",
      "intervention_strengthStandard"        ~ "Intervention: Standard",
      "intervention_strengthIntensive"       ~ "Intervention: Intensive",
      "age"                                  ~ "Age",
      "genderMale"                           ~ "Gender: Male",
      "bmi"                                  ~ "BMI",
      "physical_activity_levelModerate"      ~ "PA: Moderate",
      "physical_activity_levelHigh"          ~ "PA: High",
      "diet_score"                           ~ "Diet Score",
      "sleep_hours"                          ~ "Sleep Hours",
      "medication_adherenceModerate"         ~ "Med Adherence: Moderate",
      "medication_adherenceGood"             ~ "Med Adherence: Good",
      "comorbidity_levelMedium"              ~ "Comorbidity: Medium",
      "comorbidity_levelHigh"                ~ "Comorbidity: High",
      .default                               = term
    ),
    group = case_when(
      str_detect(term, "time_step")              ~ "Time",
      str_detect(term, "intervention_strength")  ~ "Intervention",
      term %in% c("age", "genderMale", "bmi")    ~ "Socio-demographic",
      str_detect(term, "physical_activity|diet|sleep") ~ "Lifestyle",
      str_detect(term, "medication|comorbidity") ~ "Clinical"
    ),
    significant = p.value < 0.05
  )

group_colours <- c(
  "Time"              = "#0072B2",
  "Intervention"      = "#009E73",
  "Socio-demographic" = "#E69F00",
  "Lifestyle"         = "#56B4E9",
  "Clinical"          = "#D55E00"
)

ggplot(uni_plot,
       aes(x = estimate, y = reorder(label, estimate),
           colour = group, shape = significant)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "#D55E00", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = conf.low, xmax = conf.high),
                 height = 0.3, linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = group_colours, name = "Predictor Group") +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("TRUE" = 16, "FALSE" = 1),
                     name   = "Significant (p < 0.05)") +
  labs(
    title    = "Univariate GEE Crude Coefficient Plot",
    subtitle = "Simple GEE (Exchangeable) | Crude β with 95% CI",
    x        = "Crude β (Change in HbA1c, %)",
    y        = NULL
  ) +
  theme_gee(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")
TipFigure 9 Interpretation

Lifestyle factors (sky blue) dominate the crude associations: Medication Adherence Good, Physical Activity High, diet score, and sleep hours each show large, significant crude negative effects. These will be important confounders to adjust for in the multivariable model. Gender (Male) is the only non-significant predictor (CI crosses zero, open circle), confirmed also in the multivariable model. Despite non-significance, Gender is retained as an a priori biological confounder of glycaemic control.

4.3 A: Multivariable GEE: Four Correlation Structures

Four multivariable GEE models are fitted simultaneously, each containing all predictors but with a different working correlation structure. All predictors identified in univariate screening are retained, including Gender, given its established role as a biological confounder.

Code 
gee_ind <- geeglm(
  hba1c ~ time_step + intervention_strength +
           age + gender + bmi +
           physical_activity_level + diet_score +
           sleep_hours + medication_adherence +
           comorbidity_level,
  id     = patient_id,
  data   = df,
  family = gaussian(link = "identity"),
  corstr = "independence"
)

gee_exch <- geeglm(
  hba1c ~ time_step + intervention_strength +
           age + gender + bmi +
           physical_activity_level + diet_score +
           sleep_hours + medication_adherence +
           comorbidity_level,
  id     = patient_id,
  data   = df,
  family = gaussian(link = "identity"),
  corstr = "exchangeable"
)

gee_ar1 <- geeglm(
  hba1c ~ time_step + intervention_strength +
           age + gender + bmi +
           physical_activity_level + diet_score +
           sleep_hours + medication_adherence +
           comorbidity_level,
  id     = patient_id,
  data   = df,
  family = gaussian(link = "identity"),
  corstr = "ar1"
)

gee_unstr <- geeglm(
  hba1c ~ time_step + intervention_strength +
           age + gender + bmi +
           physical_activity_level + diet_score +
           sleep_hours + medication_adherence +
           comorbidity_level,
  id     = patient_id,
  data   = df,
  family = gaussian(link = "identity"),
  corstr = "unstructured"
)

cat("All four GEE models fitted successfully.\n")
All four GEE models fitted successfully.
Code 
cat(sprintf("Unstructured α parameters: α12=%.4f  α13=%.4f  α23=%.4f\n",
            gee_unstr$geese$alpha[1],
            gee_unstr$geese$alpha[2],
            gee_unstr$geese$alpha[3]))
Unstructured α parameters: α12=0.9399  α13=0.9092  α23=0.9774

The raw summaries for each structure are presented below. These allow comparison of point estimates under different correlation assumptions before formal QIC-based selection in the next section. Inference should not be drawn from any single summary here; the QIC-selected model is the basis for final inference.

4.3.1 Independence (Baseline Reference)

Code 
summary(gee_ind)

Call:
geeglm(formula = hba1c ~ time_step + intervention_strength + 
    age + gender + bmi + physical_activity_level + diet_score + 
    sleep_hours + medication_adherence + comorbidity_level, family = gaussian(link = "identity"), 
    data = df, id = patient_id, corstr = "independence")

 Coefficients:
                                  Estimate    Std.err      Wald Pr(>|W|)    
(Intercept)                      9.8100519  0.0854323 13185.540  < 2e-16 ***
time_stepT2_Mid                 -0.3182076  0.0037667  7136.631  < 2e-16 ***
time_stepT3_Post                -0.6044971  0.0060004 10149.266  < 2e-16 ***
intervention_strengthStandard   -0.0863413  0.0210554    16.816 4.12e-05 ***
intervention_strengthIntensive  -0.2142895  0.0228679    87.811  < 2e-16 ***
age                              0.0014949  0.0005492     7.410  0.00649 ** 
genderMale                      -0.0047736  0.0162293     0.087  0.76865    
bmi                              0.0108851  0.0016768    42.142 8.49e-11 ***
physical_activity_levelModerate -0.1662461  0.0174869    90.381  < 2e-16 ***
physical_activity_levelHigh     -0.5299379  0.0201244   693.430  < 2e-16 ***
diet_score                      -0.0664025  0.0045412   213.813  < 2e-16 ***
sleep_hours                     -0.1041436  0.0077634   179.954  < 2e-16 ***
medication_adherenceModerate    -0.1431315  0.0202176    50.120 1.45e-12 ***
medication_adherenceGood        -0.5273662  0.0198546   705.508  < 2e-16 ***
comorbidity_levelMedium          0.0778810  0.0184891    17.743 2.53e-05 ***
comorbidity_levelHigh            0.1662408  0.0222979    55.584 8.96e-14 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Correlation structure = independence 
Estimated Scale Parameters:

            Estimate Std.err
(Intercept)    1.405 0.01284
Number of clusters:   20000  Maximum cluster size: 3
NoteIndependence Model Note

Independence GEE is equivalent to OLS regression with robust sandwich SE. It ignores within-patient correlation entirely, presented here as the formal baseline against which correlated structures are compared.

4.3.2 Exchangeable

Code 
summary(gee_exch)

Call:
geeglm(formula = hba1c ~ time_step + intervention_strength + 
    age + gender + bmi + physical_activity_level + diet_score + 
    sleep_hours + medication_adherence + comorbidity_level, family = gaussian(link = "identity"), 
    data = df, id = patient_id, corstr = "exchangeable")

 Coefficients:
                                 Estimate   Std.err     Wald Pr(>|W|)    
(Intercept)                     10.662525  0.084291 16001.30   <2e-16 ***
time_stepT2_Mid                 -0.215550  0.002357  8361.10   <2e-16 ***
time_stepT3_Post                -0.420470  0.003337 15874.43   <2e-16 ***
intervention_strengthStandard   -0.028192  0.024982     1.27   0.2591    
intervention_strengthIntensive  -0.076615  0.027081     8.00   0.0047 ** 
age                              0.001313  0.000648     4.10   0.0430 *  
genderMale                      -0.017066  0.019131     0.80   0.3723    
bmi                              0.086852  0.001905  2078.02   <2e-16 ***
physical_activity_levelModerate -0.194503  0.006249   968.73   <2e-16 ***
physical_activity_levelHigh     -0.515520  0.010060  2625.89   <2e-16 ***
diet_score                      -0.320496  0.003417  8795.44   <2e-16 ***
sleep_hours                     -0.405862  0.005133  6252.87   <2e-16 ***
medication_adherenceModerate    -0.175213  0.006411   746.86   <2e-16 ***
medication_adherenceGood        -0.355396  0.009841  1304.10   <2e-16 ***
comorbidity_levelMedium          0.023863  0.021718     1.21   0.2719    
comorbidity_levelHigh            0.057496  0.026192     4.82   0.0282 *  
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Correlation structure = exchangeable 
Estimated Scale Parameters:

            Estimate Std.err
(Intercept)     1.88  0.0171
  Link = identity 

Estimated Correlation Parameters:
      Estimate  Std.err
alpha    0.959 0.000465
Number of clusters:   20000  Maximum cluster size: 3

4.3.3 AR(1): Autoregressive Order 1

Code 
summary(gee_ar1)

Call:
geeglm(formula = hba1c ~ time_step + intervention_strength + 
    age + gender + bmi + physical_activity_level + diet_score + 
    sleep_hours + medication_adherence + comorbidity_level, family = gaussian(link = "identity"), 
    data = df, id = patient_id, corstr = "ar1")

 Coefficients:
                                 Estimate   Std.err     Wald Pr(>|W|)    
(Intercept)                     10.356726  0.075898 18620.24  < 2e-16 ***

time_stepT3_Post                -0.488522  0.003663 17785.10  < 2e-16 ***
intervention_strengthStandard   -0.052663  0.023033     5.23    0.022 *  
intervention_strengthIntensive  -0.132755  0.024948    28.32  1.0e-07 ***

4.3.4 Unstructured (Flexible Reference)

Code 
summary(gee_unstr)

Call:
geeglm(formula = hba1c ~ time_step + intervention_strength + 
    age + gender + bmi + physical_activity_level + diet_score + 
    sleep_hours + medication_adherence + comorbidity_level, family = gaussian(link = "identity"), 
    data = df, id = patient_id, corstr = "unstructured")

 Coefficients:

          Estimate Std.err
